institutions.institution_blurb: First president of Rollins College 1885-1892 — Florida's first institution of higher learning.
Castleton Seminary, Vermont — classical preparatory studies (Latin, Greek, mathematics) before college.
Andover Theological Seminary — graduated and ordained 1861 as Congregational minister.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Florida's first college after moving south
narrative_blocks.body: He moved from his Massachusetts parish to Winter Park, Florida in 1883 seeking better health. Within two years he was charter trustee, pastor, and first president of Rollins College — Florida's first recognized institution of higher learning.

narrative_blocks.header: Championed Seminole education in early Florida
narrative_blocks.body: As Rollins president he advocated for educational equity for Florida's Seminole population and worked to raise overall regional standards. He personally housed students in financial difficulty in his own home.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived an 1893 hurricane shipwreck refusing rescue
narrative_blocks.body: In 1893 he survived the wreck of the steamship City of Savannah, disabled by a hurricane and grounded off Fripp Island. He led prayer services on the wreck and refused rescue until others were safe; injuries from the ordeal contributed to his decline.

narrative_blocks.header: Replaced exams with daily classroom participation
narrative_blocks.body: At Rollins he introduced daily classroom participation in place of traditional examinations. He also established the gymnasium and physical education curriculum — innovations for an 1880s American college.

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker Hall at Rollins commemorates his founding role
narrative_blocks.body: The 1937 Mediterranean-style Hooker Hall — designed by Kiehnel and Elliott — bears his name. The Hooker Educational Building at Rollins College also commemorates his role as founding president (1885-1892).

cross_connections.display_label: Capt. Russell died fighting Seminoles fifty years earlier
cross_connections.description: Capt. Samuel Law Russell was killed fighting in the Second Seminole War in 1839. Roughly fifty years later, his distant cousin Rev. Edward Payson Hooker became founding president of Rollins College in Florida and was a vocal advocate for Seminole education and educational equity — a remarkable arc within one Hooker descent line.

cross_connections.display_label: also a Hooker-descended minister who led a college

career.notes: Ordained 1861 after graduating Andover Theological Seminary.

career.role: Charter trustee + College pastor + First President
career.notes: Drove the founding of Rollins College — Florida's first recognized institution of higher learning. Taught philosophy, ethics, logic, and the Bible. Introduced progressive academic practices including daily classroom participation in place of traditional exams. Established the school's gymnasium and physical education curriculum. Personally housed students in financial difficulty. Resigned 1892.

career.role: Advocate for Seminole education + regional educational standards
career.notes: Advocated for better learning opportunities for the Seminole population and worked to raise overall educational standards in the region.
Continued community service after Rollins. Survived 1893 wreck of City of Savannah; injuries led to declining health and 1898 retirement.

INST053.name: Rollins College
INST053.notes: Florida's first institution of higher learning, founded by the Florida Congregational Association. Edward Payson Hooker (H02539) was its first president 1885-1892.

INST054.name: Middlebury College
INST054.notes: Liberal arts college founded 1800 in Middlebury VT.

INST090.name: Castleton Seminary
INST090.notes: Vermont's oldest institution of higher learning (founded 1787; later reorganized as Castleton State College, now Castleton University). In the mid-19th century operated as a classical seminary preparing students for college. Edward Payson Hooker (H02539) studied Latin, Greek, and mathematics here before entering Middlebury College.

INST137.name: Andover Theological Seminary
alt_names: Andover Newton Theological School (post-1908 merger with Newton Theological Institution)
INST137.notes: Founded 1807 as a conservative Trinitarian Congregational response to Unitarian Harvard. The most prestigious Protestant theological seminary in antebellum America. Trained ministers including Rev. John Ripley Adams (X02847, 1826), Rev. Edward Strong Dwight (HD1983), and many ABCFM missionaries. Merged with Newton Theological Institution 1908 to form Andover Newton Theological School; relocated to Newton MA. Now affiliated with Yale Divinity School (as of 2017). | Merged from INST050: Founded 1808 in Andover MA. Leading Calvinist seminary. Notable Hooker-tree connection: Rev. John Erskine Edwards (HD2631) graduated 1835.
